title: Recovery Practices Checklist
description: Track your weekly recovery practices across the four evidence-based recovery experiences: psychological detachment, relaxation, mastery, and control over leisure time. Check off the practices you've done this week.
instructions: Review each item and check off the recovery practices you engaged in this week. The goal is not to check every box — it's to ensure you're getting some recovery in each of the four categories. Revisit weekly to track your progress and adjust your recovery plan.

items.label: I had a clear end-of-work time and stopped working after it
I did not check work email or messages during off-hours
I used an end-of-day transition ritual (walk, change clothes, close laptop)
When work thoughts intruded during off-hours, I redirected my attention
I spent time in nature or outdoors for enjoyment
I listened to music, a podcast, or audiobook for pleasure
I did a relaxation practice (breathing, PMR, warm bath, gentle stretching)
I had at least one evening with low stimulation (no intense TV, no scrolling)
I spent time learning something new or practicing a skill (unrelated to work)
I engaged in a creative or hands-on activity (cooking, drawing, building, writing)
I did a physical activity that challenged me in a positive way (sport, exercise, dance)
I did something absorbing that made me lose track of time (in a good way)
I had at least one evening or block of time where I chose how to spend it
I protected some free time from obligations, chores, or scheduling
I said no to a social or personal obligation that felt draining
I did something spontaneous or unplanned that I enjoyed
I connected with someone I care about (call, text, in person)
I did something purely for fun — not for productivity or achievement

disclaimer: This checklist is for self-tracking purposes. Recovery looks different for everyone — focus on what actually restores you, not on checking every box. If you are in crisis, call or text 988 (Suicide & Crisis Lifeline).
